rows.retrieval_query: What limitations or unresolved issues are reported in the target paper?

required_claims: Generalization of the laws beyond natural language is uncertain.
Predictions such as Bcrit(L) are uncertain outside the explored loss range.
The small-data regime, regularization, and data augmentation were not thoroughly studied.

answer.answer: The paper reports several limitations: low confidence in Bcrit predictions far outside explored loss range, poor fits for small data regimes, no experimentation with regularization or data augmentation, compute scaling may be confounded for large nctx, possible untuned hyperparameters, and sensitivity of learning rate to target loss. Additionally, scaling laws lack theoretical understanding and may break down at extreme scales.

claims.text: Low confidence in Bcrit predictions far outside explored loss range.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: Poor fits for small data regimes and no regularization or data augmentation experiments.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: Scaling laws lack theoretical understanding and may break down at extreme scales.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

citations.quote:
• We are not especially conﬁdent in the prediction of Bcrit(L) for values of the loss far outside the
range we have explored. Changes in Bcrit could have a signiﬁcant impact on trade-offs between
data parallelism and the number of serial training steps required, which would have a major impact
on training time.
• We did not thoroughly investigate the small data regime, and our ﬁts for L(N, D) were poor for
the smallest values of D (where an epoch corresponded to only 40 steps). Furthermore, we d

In this section we list some potential caveats to our analysis.
• At present we do not have a solid theoretical understanding for any of our proposed scaling laws.
The scaling relations with model size and compute are especially mysterious. It may be possible to
understand scaling at very large D holding model size ﬁxed [AS17], and also the shape of learning
curves late in training, by modeling the loss with a noisy quadratic. But the scaling with D at very
large model size still remains mysteri

rows.retrieval_query: How are the target paper's experiments designed and evaluated?

required_claims: Experiments use T5 checkpoints from Small through XXL.
Baselines include model tuning and multi-task model tuning.

answer.answer: Experiments use GLUE and SuperGLUE benchmarks with specified metrics, evaluating prompt tuning vs. model tuning across model sizes and domain transfer (SQuAD to MRQA). Results show prompt tuning outperforms model tuning on out-of-domain datasets, especially with large domain shifts.

rows.retrieval_query: What research problem does the target paper address?

required_claims: Full fine-tuning becomes less feasible as pre-trained models grow.
Deploying independent fine-tuned instances of GPT-3-sized models is prohibitively expensive.
The paper seeks a parameter-efficient adaptation method.

answer.answer: The target paper addresses the problem of efficiently adapting large pre-trained language models to downstream tasks without full fine-tuning, which is costly in storage and deployment.

claims.text: Full fine-tuning of large models like GPT-3 is challenging due to storage and deployment costs.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: The paper proposes a parameter-efficient approach using low-rank adaptation (LoRA).
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: Existing methods like adapter layers and prompt tuning have limitations in large-scale scenarios.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

citations.quote:
While our proposal is agnostic to training objective, we focus on language modeling as our motivat-
ing use case. Below is a brief description of the language modeling problem and, in particular, the
maximization of conditional probabilities given a task-speciﬁc prompt.
Suppose we are given a pre-trained autoregressive language model PΦ(y|x) parametrized by Φ.
For instance, PΦ(y|x) can be a generic multi-task learner such as GPT (Radford et al., b; Brown
et al., 2020) based on the Transformer ar

The problem we set out to tackle is by no means new. Since the inception of transfer learning, dozens
of works have sought to make model adaptation more parameter- and compute-efﬁcient. See Sec-
tion 6 for a survey of some of the well-known works. Using language modeling as an example, there
are two prominent strategies when it comes to efﬁcient adaptations: adding adapter layers (Houlsby
et al., 2019; Rebufﬁet al., 2017; Pfeiffer et al., 2021; R¨uckl´e et al., 2020) or optimizing some forms
of

claim_scores.required_claim: Full fine-tuning becomes less feasible as pre-trained models grow.
Deploying independent fine-tuned instances of GPT-3-sized models is prohibitively expensive.
The paper seeks a parameter-efficient adaptation method.

rows.retrieval_query: What are the target paper's main contributions?

required_claims: LoRA freezes pre-trained weights and trains low-rank matrices.
It reduces trainable parameters and GPU memory requirements.
Its linear design can be merged at inference time with no added latency.

answer.answer: The target paper proposes LoRA, a low-rank adaptation method that freezes pre-trained weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices, reducing trainable parameters by 10,000x and GPU memory by 3x for GPT-3 175B while maintaining or improving model quality.

claims.text: LoRA freezes pre-trained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into Transformer layers.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: LoRA reduces trainable parameters by 10,000 times and GPU memory by 3 times for GPT-3 175B.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: LoRA performs on-par or better than fine-tuning on RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, and GPT-3.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

citations.quote:
An important paradigm of natural language processing consists of large-scale pre-
training on general domain data and adaptation to particular tasks or domains. As
we pre-train larger models, full ﬁne-tuning, which retrains all model parameters,
becomes less feasible. Using GPT-3 175B as an example – deploying indepen-
dent instances of ﬁne-tuned models, each with 175B parameters, is prohibitively
expensive. We propose Low-Rank Adaptation, or LoRA, which freezes the pre-
trained model weights an

claim_scores.required_claim: LoRA freezes pre-trained weights and trains low-rank matrices.
It reduces trainable parameters and GPU memory requirements.
Its linear design can be merged at inference time with no added latency.

rows.retrieval_query: What limitations or unresolved issues are reported in the target paper?

required_claims: The mechanism behind fine-tuning and LoRA remains unclear.
Selecting which matrices to adapt currently depends largely on heuristics.
Combining LoRA with other efficient adaptation methods remains a future direction.

answer.answer: The target paper reports that existing adapter layers introduce inference latency and prompt tuning reduces usable sequence length, while LoRA cannot batch inputs with different modules in a single forward pass when weights are merged.

claims.text: Adapter layers introduce inference latency in large-scale production scenarios.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: Prompt tuning reduces the usable sequence length for downstream tasks.

claims.text: LoRA cannot batch inputs with different A and B in a single forward pass when weights are merged.

citations.quote:
The problem we set out to tackle is by no means new. Since the inception of transfer learning, dozens
of works have sought to make model adaptation more parameter- and compute-efﬁcient. See Sec-
tion 6 for a survey of some of the well-known works. Using language modeling as an example, there
are two prominent strategies when it comes to efﬁcient adaptations: adding adapter layers (Houlsby
et al., 2019; Rebufﬁet al., 2017; Pfeiffer et al., 2021; R¨uckl´e et al., 2020) or optimizing some forms
of

LoRA also has its limitations. For example, it is not straightforward to batch inputs to different tasks
with different A and B in a single forward pass, if one chooses to absorb A and B into W to eliminate
additional inference latency. Though it is possible to not merge the weights and dynamically choose
the LoRA modules to use for samples in a batch for scenarios where latency is not critical.

Directly Optimizing the Prompt is Hard
The other direction, as exempliﬁed by preﬁx tuning (Li
& Liang, 2021), faces a different challenge. We observe that preﬁx tuning is difﬁcult to optimize
and that its performance changes non-monotonically in trainable parameters, conﬁrming similar
observations in the original paper. More fundamentally, reserving a part of the sequence length for
adaptation necessarily reduces the sequence length available to process a downstream task, which
we suspect makes

claim_scores.required_claim: The mechanism behind fine-tuning and LoRA remains unclear.
Selecting which matrices to adapt currently depends largely on heuristics.

citations.quote:
5.1
Implications for alignment research
This research is part of our broader research program to align AI systems with human intentions (Chris-
tiano et al., 2017; Ziegler et al., 2019; Stiennon et al., 2020). Even though this work focuses on
our current language model systems, we seek general and scalable methods that work for future AI
systems (Leike et al., 2018). The systems we work with here are still fairly limited, but they are
among the largest language models today and we apply them on

Research on alignment and learning from human feedback.
We build on previous techniques
to align models with human intentions, particularly reinforcement learning from human feed-
back (RLHF). Originally developed for training simple robots in simulated environments and Atari
games (Christiano et al., 2017; Ibarz et al., 2018), it has recently been applied to ﬁne-tuning language
models to summarize text (Ziegler et al., 2019; Stiennon et al., 2020; Böhm et al., 2019; Wu et al.,
2021). This work

rows.retrieval_query: What are the target paper's main contributions?

required_claims: The paper fine-tunes language models with human demonstrations and preferences.
InstructGPT outputs are preferred over GPT-3 outputs in human evaluations.
The paper reports improvements in truthfulness and some toxicity measures.

answer.answer: The target paper's main contributions are: (1) fine-tuning large language models using human preferences significantly improves their behavior on a wide range of tasks, and (2) InstructGPT models are preferred over GPT-3 outputs by labelers, with the 1.3B InstructGPT outperforming the 175B GPT-3.

claims.text: Fine-tuning large language models using human preferences significantly improves their behavior on a wide range of tasks.

claims.text: InstructGPT models are preferred over GPT-3 outputs by labelers.

claims.text: The 1.3B InstructGPT outperforms the 175B GPT-3 despite having over 100x fewer parameters.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

citations.quote:
InstructGPT still makes simple mistakes.
For example, InstructGPT can still fail to follow
instructions, make up facts, give long hedging answers to simple questions, or fail to detect instructions

Overall, our results indicate that ﬁne-tuning large language models using human preferences signiﬁ-
cantly improves their behavior on a wide range of tasks, though much work remains to be done to
improve their safety and reliability.

answer.answer: The experiments evaluate Flan-PaLM on held-out benchmarks (MMLU, BBH, TyDiQA, MGSM) using direct and chain-of-thought prompting, plus a human evaluation on 190 open-ended examples. Scaling curves show improvements from larger models and more finetuning tasks.

citations.quote:
Evaluation benchmarks. We focus on performance on held-out tasks which were not included as part of the
ﬁnetuning data. We are interested in Flan-PaLM’s overall capabilities on world knowledge and reasoning
tasks. Thus, we evaluate the model on a range of diﬀerent benchmarks, including multilingual ones. We
do not use the evaluation set from Brown et al. (2020) since almost all of those tasks have training sets that
are included in our ﬁnetuning mixture. Instead, we use the following challenging

Beyond the NLP benchmarks, language models are also capable of generating long-form answers to open-
ended requests. Standard NLP benchmarks and the automatic metrics used to evaluate them are not suﬃcient
to measure human preferences among these open-form responses (Ouyang et al., 2022). Hence, we conduct
a manual evaluation that investigates the eﬀect of instruction ﬁnetuning on the ability for models to give
open-ended responses to challenging inputs. To do this, we created an evaluation set

Evaluation methods and metrics. For MMLU and BBH, we evaluate both the ability to directly predict the
answer via direct prompting, where the model directly gives the answer (Brown et al., 2020; Srivastava et al.,
2022), as well as via chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, where the model must provide a reasoning chain
before giving the ﬁnal answer (Wei et al., 2022b). For TyDiQA, we only measure direct prompting exact-match
score, since highlighting the portion of a passage with the correct answer

temperature sampling with τ = 0.7 to generate ﬁve responses randomly, and then rank them by log probability
score without length normalization. We choose the response with the best score, after a ﬁltering step of
removing any generations with scores that were better than half of the median score, which we found
successfully removed a large portion of generations with undesirable repetitions. For example, if the median
log probability score of ﬁve generations is -20, then a generation with a scor

citations.quote:
The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or
convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best
performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention
mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer,
based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions
entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to
be superior in quality while being mor

rows.retrieval_query: What limitations or unresolved issues are reported in the target paper?

required_claims: The work is primarily a survey/exploration rather than a new-method proposal.
The coordinate-ascent study can miss second-order effects among design choices.
Some automatic benchmark improvements may not correspond to better qualitative behavior.

answer.answer: The paper reports that English-only pre-training did not achieve state-of-the-art on translation tasks, and that scale and English-language pre-training may be insufficient to match sophisticated methods like backtranslation. Additionally, improvements to ROUGE scores do not necessarily correspond to more coherent summaries, and generative models trained with maximum likelihood are prone to repetitive summaries. The paper also notes that a more rigorous notion of task similarity would be useful for principled pre-training data selection.

claims.text: English-only pre-training did not achieve state-of-the-art on translation tasks.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: Scale and English-language pre-training may be insufficient to match sophisticated methods like backtranslation.

claims.text: Improvements to ROUGE scores do not necessarily correspond to more coherent summaries.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

citations.quote:
We did not achieve state-of-the-art performance on any of the WMT translation tasks.
This may be in part due to our use of an English-only unlabeled data set. We also note that
most of the best results on these tasks use backtranslation (Edunov et al., 2018; Lample and
Conneau, 2019), which is a sophisticated data augmentation scheme. The state of the art on
the low-resource English to Romanian benchmark also uses additional forms of cross-lingual
unsupervised training (Lample and Conneau, 2019)

required_claims: The evaluation compares GPT-3 with seven smaller models.

answer.answer: The experiments train 8 GPT-3 models (125M to 175B parameters) on a filtered Common Crawl and curated datasets, then evaluate on 9 task categories in zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot settings using metrics like cross-entropy loss, accuracy, F1, BLEU, and exact match.

rows.retrieval_query: Compare the main contributions of the two target papers.

required_claims: The Transformer paper introduces an attention-only architecture without recurrence.
The BERT paper introduces bidirectional Transformer pre-training with masked language modeling.
BERT demonstrates broad downstream gains through fine-tuning, while Transformer focuses on sequence transduction architecture.

answer.answer: The Transformer introduces a novel architecture based solely on attention, replacing recurrence and convolutions, enabling greater parallelization and state-of-the-art translation quality. BERT demonstrates the importance of bidirectional pre-training using masked language models, achieving state-of-the-art on eleven NLP tasks.

claims.text: Transformer is based solely on attention mechanisms, eschewing recurrence and convolutions.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: Transformer achieves state-of-the-art translation quality with greater parallelization.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

claims.text: BERT uses masked language models for bidirectional pre-training, advancing state-of-the-art on eleven NLP tasks.
claims.support_note: citation identifiers validated against supplied context

citations.quote:
Recurrent neural networks, long short-term memory [13] and gated recurrent [7] neural networks
in particular, have been firmly established as state of the art approaches in sequence modeling and
transduction problems such as language modeling and machine translation [35, 2, 5]. Numerous
efforts have since continued to push the boundaries of recurrent language models and encoder-decoder

In this work, we presented the Transformer, the first sequence transduction model based entirely on
attention, replacing the recurrent layers most commonly used in encoder-decoder architectures with
multi-headed self-attention.
For translation tasks, the Transformer can be trained significantly faster than architectures based
on recurrent or convolutional layers. On both WMT 2014 English-to-German and WMT 2014
English-to-French translation tasks, we achieve a new state of the art. In the former

The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or
convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best
performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention
mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer,
based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions
entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to
be superior in quality while being mor

claim_scores.required_claim: The Transformer paper introduces an attention-only architecture without recurrence.
The BERT paper introduces bidirectional Transformer pre-training with masked language modeling.
BERT demonstrates broad downstream gains through fine-tuning, while Transformer focuses on sequence transduction architecture.

rows.retrieval_query: Within the attention-only Transformer sequence transduction paper, what exact total energy consumption is reported for all experiments?

required_claims: No exact total energy-consumption value is reported in the paper.

answer.refusal_reason: The paper does not report a total energy consumption for all experiments; it only mentions training costs in terms of time and GPU count for specific tasks.
